retrieved_docs.title: Syringomyelia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The presentation of syringomyelia is highly variable, with patients reporting symptoms such as pain, muscle weakness, particularly in the hands and arms, temperature insensitivity in the upper limb, spasticity or stiffness in the lower limbs, and progressive scoliosis. The clinical course typically progresses over months to years, initially experiencing rapid deterioration that later slows down. [5] A linear relationship is observed between cyst morphology, symptom duration, and severity. [38] [39] In cases where associated CM-1 is present, patients may also experience occipital headaches (precipitated by coughing, straining, and sneezing), neck pain, gait, balance problems, dizziness, hoarseness, and swallowing difficulties, as well as sleep disturbances such as snoring.

retrieved_docs.title: Syringomyelia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical manifestations, neurological symptoms, and predisposing conditions associated with syringomyelia, such as Chiari malformation, spinal cord trauma, or tumors. Screen patients with relevant medical history or presenting symptoms for potential syringomyelia, including those with spinal cord injuries or congenital abnormalities. Assess the progression of syringomyelia using appropriate imaging techniques and clinical evaluations. Collaborate with other healthcare professionals to optimize patient outcomes and coordinate comprehensive care plans for individuals with syringomyelia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Myelomeningocele -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Myelomeningocele is a severe neural tube defect resulting from incomplete closure of the spinal cord during early fetal development, usually within the first month of gestation. This condition is a form of open spinal dysraphism or spina bifida aperta. Myelomeningocele is characterized by an exposed spinal cord and meninges that protrude from the vertebral column, which is evident during a physical examination. The severity of neurological impairments varies depending on the lesion's location along the spine. Higher lesions often cause motor deficits, such as paraplegia, while lower lesions may lead to sensory loss and bladder or bowel dysfunction. Risk factors for myelomeningocele include environmental exposures, maternal health issues, inadequate pre-pregnancy folic acid intake, and genetic factors, including chromosomal abnormalities such as trisomy 18 or 13.

retrieved_docs.title: Cauda Equina and Conus Medullaris Syndromes -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis centers on non-compressible causes of spinal cord dysfunction such as: Spinal cord infarct HIV-related myelopathy Transverse myelitis Multiple sclerosis Syringomyelia Spinal arteriovenous malformation

retrieved_docs.title: Syringomyelia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The natural history of patients with syringomyelia is variable and unpredictable, with periods of stability and progression. Although professionals do not completely understand the natural history of syringomyelia, the clinical course typically progresses over months to years, with an early rapid deterioration that gradually slows down. [5] Sudden head jolting or prolonged bouts of coughing can trigger a sudden onset of symptoms in a previously asymptomatic patient, presumably because of an increase in tonsillar descent. [6] [7] Notably, syringomyelia accounts for up to 5% of paraplegia. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Tethered Cord Syndrome (TCS) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the pathophysiology of tethered cord syndrome. Describe the clinical presentation of tethered cord syndrome. Identify the radiographic appearance of tethered cord syndrome. Outline the evaluation and treatment/management of tethered cord syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Syringomyelia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Although professionals do not completely understand the natural history of syringomyelia, the clinical course or symptoms typically progress over months to years, with an early rapid deterioration that gradually slows down. Sudden head jolting or prolonged bouts of coughing can trigger abrupt symptom onset in previously asymptomatic individuals due to increased tonsillar descent. Managing symptoms involves avoiding triggers such as straining or neck flexion to reduce venous pressure and alleviate symptoms, thereby highlighting the necessity for an interdisciplinary approach to treat and care for patients with syringomyelia. This activity focuses on addressing the presentation, evaluation, and management of syringomyelia in the affected patients. This activity also explores the interprofessional collaboration of the healthcare team in caring for patients affected by this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Tethered Cord Syndrome (TCS) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If TCS is suspected based on clinical presentation, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine is the radiographic modality of choice. MRI aids in demonstrating the level of the conus medullaris, identifying lesions associated with the tethered spinal cord, and surgical planning. Despite the variability, the normal level of the conus is at or above the second lumbar level. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Tethered Cord Syndrome (TCS) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Tethered cord syndrome is a well-recognized cause of deterioration in the myelomeningocele population, with an estimated one-third of patients requiring spinal cord untethering in childhood. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Tethered Cord Syndrome (TCS) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: MRI can further aid in visualizing the underlying pathology associated with the spinal cord tethering, including myelomeningocele, lipomyelomeningocele, split cord malformation, tumor, etc. Constructive interference in steady state (CISS) MRI sequence may be used in indeterminate cases as it has been shown to have superior sensitivity to T2-weighted MRI sequences. [23] In addition to MRI, standing scoliosis X-rays and computed tomography (CT) scans can also be considered to further evaluate scoliosis if present.

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Syringomyelia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: For individuals diagnosed with PTS, education should emphasize treatment options, self-care strategies, and the importance of adhering to medical recommendations. Patients undergoing surgical intervention should understand the risks, benefits, and expected outcomes of procedures such as cord untethering, syrinx-subarachnoid shunting, or decompressive surgery. They should also be informed about potential recurrence and the need for long-term monitoring. By fostering patient engagement and shared decision-making, clinicians can enhance adherence to follow-up care and improve overall outcomes in PTS management.

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Syringomyelia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients’ self-reports on changes in their functional abilities—such as walking, wheelchair propulsion, and transfers—are often the most significant indicators of the condition's progression. Additionally, individuals with cervical syringomyelia should be monitored for pulmonary function to assess any decline in vital capacity. Interdisciplinary evaluations by rehabilitation teams can help determine adjustments needed for mobility devices, seating, walking activities, and daily living tasks. This team approach aims to reduce the risks of complications such as pressure ulcers, decreased mobility, and falls.

retrieved_docs.content: Myelomeningocele can be associated with Chiari type II malformation, hydrocephalus, and tethered spinal cord.

retrieved_docs.title: Spina Bifida -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses of spina bifida are generally straightforward based on the physical presentation. However, when clinical or radiographic findings are equivocal, several differential diagnoses should be considered, including: Cord compression Diastematomyelia Isolated Chiari malformations Mass lesions Tethered cord

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Conus Medullaris -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Tethered cord syndrome is a condition caused by an abnormal filum terminale that prevents the conus medullaris from ascending and restricts the mobility of the spinal cord. As a result, the spinal cord gets abnormally stretched, with neurologic symptoms referable to the lower cord. [17] It is typically diagnosed during periods of rapid growth in adolescence but may go undiagnosed until adulthood. MRI shows a low lying conus, i.e., below the L2 vertebra or a thickened filum terminale. However, Tubbs and Oaks studied a cohort of patients of tethered cord syndrome in whom the conus was in the normal range and suggested that this condition might be tautness of the cord rather than pure distal elongation. [18] Frequently, it is associated with spinal anomalies like diastematomyelia, dermal sinuses, and lipomas. Treatment is via surgical release and halts the progression of symptoms. According to Liu et al., spinal anesthesia should be avoided in adults with tethered cord syndrome to prevent long term neurologic damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق جافوي is closely associated with the dura mater, as the dura mater serves as the primary site where findings of تمزق جافوي are observed. In addition, تمزق جافوي functions as an antecedent or parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that the presence of تمزق جافوي may predispose patients to subsequent spinal cord injury. Recognizing the involvement of the dura mater in تمزق جافوي and its potential role in the development of spinal cord injury is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a condition that manifests during the fetal period. The fetal period itself is linked to other prenatal abnormalities, including wanverhouding; foetale ascites and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal hydrocephalus causing disproportion). Recognizing that fetal disproportion associated with myelomeningocele, as well as ascites and hydrocephalus, all occur within the same developmental window underscores the importance of comprehensive fetal assessment to identify and manage multiple concurrent anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2149
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a prenatal growth abnormality that manifests during the fetal period. In the same developmental window, other fluid‑related fetal conditions also arise, including wanverhouding with foetale ascites, wanverhouding with foetale hydrops, and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal cerebral hydrocephalus causing disproportion). These entities are all linked by their occurrence in the fetal period, reflecting a spectrum of fetal fluid accumulation and structural imbalance that can be identified through prenatal imaging and require coordinated obstetric assessment and management.

Overall, the network of relationships shows that MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY integrates fetal developmental disorders (such as myelomeningocele, hydrocephalus, and fluid accumulations), maternal pelvic dynamics (pelvic inlet, outlet, and overall contraction), and standard delivery practices (normal delivery procedure and spontaneous vaginal delivery). Recognizing how these entities interrelate is essential for clinicians to assess risk, plan appropriate interventions, and optimize outcomes for both mother and fetus during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Injury of central nervous system due to birth trauma is a condition in which the pathological finding is located within the central nervous system. The central nervous system is anatomically linked to the dura mater, as the dura mater constitutes a physical component of the protective meningeal layers that surround the brain and spinal cord. Consequently, injuries to the central nervous system, such as those caused by birth trauma, involve structures that are encased by the dura mater, underscoring the importance of the dura mater in shielding the central nervous system and influencing clinical assessment and management of such injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 834
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that encompasses a variety of specific fetal and obstetric entities, procedural concepts, and anatomical factors relevant to the process of childbirth. Within this category, the condition Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation is listed, indicating a successful repositioning of a breech fetus to a head‑down orientation prior to delivery. The category also includes Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele, a neural‑tube defect that can cause size mismatch between the fetus and the maternal pelvis, and this specific condition is situated within the Fetal period, highlighting its occurrence during fetal development. Several fetal abnormalities that affect labor are enumerated as child concepts of MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is described as a physical component of the dura mater, indicating its anatomical relationship within the meningeal coverings. In clinical assessment, the central nervous system serves as the finding site for central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي), highlighting its relevance as the location where such pathological findings are identified. Understanding that the central nervous system is both anatomically linked to the dura mater and is the primary site of CNS injuries informs diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in neurology and neurosurgery.

Consequently, a cervical spine fracture that involves the spinal cord can lead to direct tissue injury that may produce neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of addressing both structural damage and its potential neurotoxic consequences in patient management.
